content:
The loss-surface idea grows out of **calculus of variations** and **multivariate optimization**, where objective functions over continuous parameters were studied as surfaces long before machine learning.

In classical statistics, **least squares** (Gauss, Legendre) made the parabolic error bowl concrete: a smooth, convex landscape with a closed-form minimum.

As **nonlinear models** (logistic regression, neural networks) gained traction in the 20th century, researchers could no longer rely on a single global bowl. Early neural-net training folklore emphasized **local minima** as the enemy; later work highlighted **saddles** and high-dimensional geometry as more representative obstacles.

The term **loss landscape** became common in deep-learning research (2010s onward) as papers visualized 2D slices, mode connectivity, and flat minima to explain why stochastic gradient methods work despite non-convexity.

Today the metaphor is standard pedagogy: define L(θ), picture terrain over θ, and place optimizers as paths on that terrain—bridging 19th-century optimization geometry and modern large-scale training.

timeline.label: Least squares geometry
timeline.description: Quadratic error as a convex bowl establishes the simplest loss surface with a unique minimum.

timeline.label: Nonlinear optimization
timeline.description: General multivariate objectives introduce local minima, constraints, and numerical methods beyond closed forms.

timeline.label: Neural network landscapes
timeline.description: Empirical contour plots and critical-point theory refocus debate from 'bad local minima' to saddles, flat regions, and path-dependent solutions.

timeline.label: Modern deep learning practice
timeline.description: Landscape visualizations inform learning-rate warmup, sharpness-aware minimization, and generalization research in large models.
